impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in granting judgment as a matter of law that Teva's headache patents claiming methods of treating headache using humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's JMOL of invalidity, reinstating a jury verdict that Eli Lilly willfully infringed Teva's patents covering anti-CGRP antibody treatments for headache. This case involves blockbuster migraine drugs (Teva's Ajovy) and has major implications for the scope of antibody patent claims in the pharmaceutical industry.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical and biotech companies holding broad antibody patents benefit from the ruling, as it validates genus claims for antibodies where the specification discloses representative species and known methods of making additional species.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors developing biosimilar or next-generation antibody therapies face heightened infringement risk where pioneer patents claim broad classes of humanized antibodies for therapeutic methods.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the tied products had substantial non-infringing uses that would preclude antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act for conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the antitrust damages award, finding sufficient evidence that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent's scope.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors benefit from this ruling, which limits patent holders' ability to leverage patents to foreclose competition in adjacent unpatented product markets through bundled licensing arrangements.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that bundle patent licenses with product purchase requirements face significant antitrust liability risk, particularly where the tied products have uses outside the scope of the patent.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are directed to an abstract idea under step one of the Alice framework, and whether damages for foreign sales of infringing software are recoverable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict, holding that Columbia's cybersecurity patents were directed to abstract ideas under Alice step one, requiring remand on step two. The court also ruled that damages based on Norton's foreign software sales were improperly awarded, reinforcing extraterritoriality limits on patent damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing patent infringement claims based on cybersecurity and software detection methods gain a stronger basis to challenge such patents under § 101, and foreign sales are shielded from U.S. patent damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in software and cybersecurity fields face increased vulnerability to § 101 challenges, and universities and research institutions may see reduced damages awards where accused products are sold globally.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content presentation specifications to a wireless device's capabilities before delivering the content are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming media patents were ineligible under § 101, reversing a $2.5 million jury verdict for GoTV against Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to device capabilities, requiring entry of judgment for Netflix and invalidating the patents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies benefit from this ruling, which limits the enforceability of broadly claimed content-adaptation patents and reduces litigation exposure from patent assertion entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims covering content adaptation and delivery methods face heightened § 101 invalidity risk, particularly where claims recite conventional server-client interactions.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder can establish infringement of method and apparatus claims for multicore processor technology where the accused acts of designing and testing occur in the United States but the chips are manufactured and sold abroad, and whether doctrine of equivalents may overcome literal non-infringement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed in part the district court's grant of summary judgment of noninfringement to Intel on extraterritoriality grounds, finding genuine disputes of material fact as to whether Intel's domestic design and testing activities constituted infringing use of the patented multicore processor selection technology. The court affirmed on other grounds.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting semiconductor and processor patents benefit from the holding that domestic design and testing activities may constitute actionable infringement even when manufacturing occurs abroad.
impacts.negativeImplications: Semiconductor companies and other firms with global manufacturing operations face expanded infringement exposure for U.S. patent claims based on domestic R&D activities even where products are fabricated overseas.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was properly granted on inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims related to patents on quench-and-tempered coiled tubing, where the patentee allegedly withheld prior art documents acquired through a corporate acquisition.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact as to whether Tenaris withheld material prior art (the CYMAX documents) from the PTO during prosecution and whether that conduct supported attempted monopolization. The case was remanded for trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors benefit from the ruling's reaffirmation that companies acquiring prior art through asset purchases must disclose it during patent prosecution, and that failure to do so may expose patentees to both inequitable conduct and antitrust liability.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that obtain patents after acquiring predecessor companies' assets face significant litigation risk if prior art from the acquired entity is not disclosed to the PTO during prosecution.

impacts.caseName: International Medical Devices, Inc. v. Cornell
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the plaintiff presented legally sufficient evidence that its asserted trade secrets regarding a cosmetic penile implant were protectable trade secrets under California law, including that the information was not generally known.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the denial of JMOL on trade secret misappropriation claims, finding insufficient evidence that the asserted trade secrets were protectable because the information was generally known in the field. The court also reversed the patent invalidity finding tied to the same inventive-contribution analysis, vacated the permanent injunction and exemplary damages, but affirmed counterfeiting liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in trade secret cases benefit from the heightened scrutiny applied to whether asserted information was truly secret rather than generally known, providing stronger grounds for JMOL challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret holders face greater difficulty sustaining misappropriation claims where the underlying information overlaps with publicly available knowledge, even if specific implementations are proprietary.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether Rule 30(b)(6) corporate deposition testimony from a witness lacking personal knowledge is admissible at summary judgment to establish the on-sale bar under pre-AIA 35 U.S.C. § 102(b), and whether source code constitutes hearsay.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of invalidity under the on-sale bar, holding that 30(b)(6) deposition testimony is admissible at summary judgment regardless of the witness's personal knowledge, and that source code commands are not hearsay statements. This provides important evidentiary guidance for proving prior art sales in patent litigation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers benefit from expanded ability to use corporate representative testimony and source code evidence to prove on-sale bar invalidity defenses, even when original engineers are unavailable.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased invalidity risk from the on-sale bar where corporate successors can testify about historical product sales through 30(b)(6) witnesses without personal knowledge.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features infringed Masimo's patents on wearable pulse oximetry devices, and whether Masimo satisfied the domestic industry requirement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's limited exclusion order barring importation of Apple Watch models that infringed Masimo's wearable blood oxygen measurement patents. The court upheld the Commission's findings on validity, infringement, and domestic industry, sustaining one of the most commercially significant ITC exclusion orders in recent years.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders enforcing technology patents at the ITC benefit from the affirmation that the domestic industry requirement can be satisfied through significant R&D investments, and that exclusion orders remain a powerful remedy against large importers.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major technology companies importing products with features covered by third-party patents face continued risk of ITC exclusion orders that can block entire product lines from the U.S. market.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled district court declaratory judgment action when it missed the deadline for seeking a stay of the original action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that an ITC respondent cannot circumvent the statutory deadline for obtaining a mandatory stay of a parallel district court action under § 1659(a) by dismissing and refiling a declaratory judgment action to restart the clock. This prevents gamesmanship in the coordination of ITC and district court patent proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical and technology companies seeking prompt district court adjudication of patent disputes (particularly safe harbor defenses) benefit from the ruling, which prevents opponents from using refiled actions to delay court proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents that miss the 30-day deadline for seeking a mandatory stay lose the ability to obtain one through procedural maneuvering, potentially forcing them to litigate on two fronts simultaneously.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy where the expert relied on the benefits derived from the patented technology.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft's Azure platform, holding the district court applied an overly rigid standard to the expert's methodology. The court vacated summary judgment entered solely on the basis of no viable damages theory, restoring the infringement case.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the ruling's more permissive approach to damages expert testimony, making it harder for defendants to obtain case-dispositive exclusions of damages evidence at the Daubert stage.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent suits face the prospect of more damages theories surviving Daubert challenges, potentially increasing settlement pressure and trial exposure.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to the PACER system, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were permissible.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that PACER fees exceeded statutory authority. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to the three nonprofit class representatives, providing guidance on class settlement approval standards for government fee challenges.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that are heavy PACER users benefit from the settlement providing full or substantial reimbursement of overcharged fees, and the precedent supports future challenges to excessive government user fees.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed 'encryption information' in claims directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the Board's claim construction was consistent with the specification and prosecution history.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction in an IPR involving DivX's streaming encryption patent, adopting a narrower construction and vacating the Board's decision that Netflix failed to prove obviousness. The case was remanded for the Board to reassess obviousness under the corrected claim construction.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and content delivery companies benefit from the reversal, which may ultimately invalidate a patent covering partially encrypted adaptive bitrate streaming technology widely used in the industry.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital media streaming space face the risk of claim constructions that narrow the scope of encryption-related patent claims based on specification disclosures.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute, and whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a abrogates the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket confidentiality designation for all questionnaire responses in trade remedy investigations is incompatible with the statute. The court held that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial proceedings, requiring the ITC to evaluate confidentiality on a case-by-case basis.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers, domestic producers, and the public benefit from greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, potentially enabling better-informed participation in antidumping and countervailing duty cases.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting sensitive business data in ITC questionnaires face increased risk of disclosure, as the ITC can no longer automatically designate all responses as confidential without case-specific justification.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding Board discretionary non-institution decisions in IPR proceedings are general policy statements exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized the Director's unreviewable statutory discretion over institution decisions and the non-binding nature of the instructions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the ruling, which preserves the PTO Director's broad discretion to decline IPR institution through internal policy guidance without the procedural burdens of formal rulemaking.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and frequent IPR petitioners face continued uncertainty from non-institution decisions guided by internal policy pronouncements that are not subject to public comment or judicial review on the merits.
